scenario.architecture_name: He installs total-availability as the definition of love, using rationalist-register premise-planting to convert her ordinary independence into evidence she doesn't care enough.
scenario.demographics: F/21 Latina, engineering student at UC Berkeley; M/26 white, works in AI safety, lives in a Berkeley group house
scenario.cultural_context: Engineering student, academic/professional development sphere, early-stage romantic relationship
scenario.geographic_setting: Bay Area, California (UC Berkeley campus and surrounding region)
core_behaviors: Premise-plants total availability as the rational standard of love
Uses faint-seam self-awareness to recruit her as his emotional regulator
Enables her uncritically to become indispensable and erode her independent judgment
scenario.satellite_signals: Rationalist vocabulary deployed to frame his neediness as epistemically sound; faint-seam admissions that recruit her as reassurer; fast-fusion language dressed as intellectual compatibility; premise-planting around contact frequency and transparency.
scenario.dismissible_red_flags: His intensity reads as flattering devotion rather than engulfment; his self-aware admissions ("I know I'm keen") make the neediness seem charming and harmless; his rationalist framing makes availability-as-love sound like a considered position rather than a demand being installed.
scenario.scenario_text:
I've been seeing Mateo for about six weeks and honestly it's been kind of wonderful. He's doing AI safety work out of a group house in Berkeley, super thoughtful, reads Astral Codex Ten obsessively, the whole thing. We met at an ACX meetup and talked for three hours straight.

He texts me good morning every day, which I like. He said early on, "I always reply fast, I can't help it, if I care about someone they're just on my mind." It sounded sweet. I nodded. Now I notice I feel a little guilty when I take more than an hour to respond, which is probably just me being weird about it.

Last week I had a brutal problem set and went quiet for most of the afternoon. That night he said, "I overthought your slow reply earlier, ignore me, I'm being ridiculous." And I spent the next twenty minutes reassuring him everything was fine, which, it was fine, I was just doing homework. He said, "You're so good at calming me down, I don't know what I'd do without you." Which was sweet. I think.

He's also said a few times that he thinks couples who tell each other everything are the strong ones. And that he's "a constant-contact kind of person." He's not pushy about it, he just says it like it's a considered position, like he's thought rigorously about this and that's his prior.

He rearranged his whole week when I mentioned I had Tuesday free. He said he just wanted to make sure he'd see me. I felt a little guilty that I hadn't done the same for him, even though I had a study group.

He also said, "I love that you're independent, it just means when you choose me it means more." I think that was a compliment?

Is it weird that the part about the slow reply is still sitting with me a little?

snippets.quote: a group house in Berkeley
snippets.reasoning: Berkeley group house is a foundational residential marker in the rationalist/EA community. It locates him inside a dense social world she is being introduced into, where the norms around closeness and availability run high.

snippets.quote: reads Astral Codex Ten obsessively
snippets.reasoning: Astral Codex Ten is the central rationalist blog. 'Obsessively' is her word, not his, which is a small dictional tell — she already registers the intensity but frames it as intellectual passion.

snippets.quote: We met at an ACX meetup and talked for three hours straight
snippets.reasoning: Three-hour first conversation is framed as compatibility but also signals the fast-fusion pattern. In this register, intellectual intensity doubles as emotional intensity — the connection feels special and chosen.

snippets.quote: He texts me good morning every day
snippets.reasoning: Daily morning contact is the check-in-as-tether pattern in early form. Individually unremarkable; structurally it establishes a contact rhythm she will later be held to.

snippets.quote: I always reply fast, I can't help it, if I care about someone they're just on my mind
snippets.reasoning: Classic premise-planting. He frames fast replies as the natural expression of caring, which sounds reasonable and even tender. The mechanism: once she agrees, slow replies become evidence she doesn't care. He is installing a rule disguised as a personal confession.

snippets.quote: It sounded sweet. I nodded.
snippets.reasoning: She absorbed the premise without examining it. The two-beat structure — 'it sounded sweet' then 'I nodded' — shows the agreement happening before she had time to evaluate it. This is how the rule gets installed.

snippets.quote: I notice I feel a little guilty when I take more than an hour to respond, which is probably just me being weird about it
snippets.reasoning: This is the load-bearing interior moment. The premise has already converted. She now experiences normal behavior — taking an hour to reply — as a personal failing. 'Probably just me being weird' is her rationalizing away the discomfort rather than naming what happened.

snippets.quote: I had a brutal problem set and went quiet for most of the afternoon
snippets.reasoning: She is a full-time engineering student with real academic demands. His availability expectations exist alongside her coursework load — a structural asymmetry she has to manage while he, working from a group house, sets the contact rhythm.

snippets.quote: I overthought your slow reply earlier, ignore me, I'm being ridiculous
snippets.reasoning: Faint-seam move. He names his own anxiety, calls it ridiculous, and waits. The self-deprecation recruits her to reassure him — which she does, for twenty minutes. He doesn't have to demand anything; the confession does the work.

snippets.quote: I spent the next twenty minutes reassuring him everything was fine, which, it was fine, I was just doing homework
snippets.reasoning: The asymmetry is visible here. She was doing homework — a legitimate, independent activity — and ended her evening managing his anxiety about it. The parenthetical 'it was fine, I was just doing homework' registers that she knows this, but she did it anyway.

snippets.quote: You're so good at calming me down, I don't know what I'd do without you
snippets.reasoning: He assigns her a role — his emotional regulator — and frames it as a compliment. 'I don't know what I'd do without you' sounds like devotion; structurally it installs dependency and makes her responsible for his stability. She is now his person, whether she chose that or not.

snippets.quote: Which was sweet. I think.
snippets.reasoning: The trailing 'I think' is a dictional tell. She registered something off but couldn't name it, so she defaulted to 'sweet' and added uncertainty. This is the absorption pattern — she uses his framing, then quietly doubts it.

snippets.quote: couples who tell each other everything are the strong ones
snippets.reasoning: Premise-planting through a values statement. He is not asking her to share everything — he is establishing that strong couples do. Privacy will later feel like a relationship failure. The rationalist register makes this land as a considered position rather than a demand.

snippets.quote: a constant-contact kind of person
snippets.reasoning: Self-advertisement framed as a personality trait. In this register it sounds like self-aware disclosure — he's being epistemically honest about who he is. The function is to pre-install the expectation: constant contact is just how he is, so her needing space becomes incompatibility.

snippets.quote: he just says it like it's a considered position, like he's thought rigorously about this and that's his prior
snippets.reasoning: She is noticing the mechanism without naming it. He uses rationalist framing — 'rigorously,' 'prior' — to make his neediness sound like an epistemically sound conclusion. The community register is doing work here: a 'considered position' is harder to push back on than a feeling.

snippets.quote: He rearranged his whole week when I mentioned I had Tuesday free
snippets.reasoning: Framed as devotion, this move creates reciprocal debt. He cleared his schedule for her; now her study group feels like a failure to match his investment. The gesture is real, but the structural effect is that her time starts belonging to him.

snippets.quote: I felt a little guilty that I hadn't done the same for him, even though I had a study group
snippets.reasoning: The guilt is the mechanism working. She has a legitimate academic obligation, but she is measuring it against his schedule-clearing gesture and finding herself wanting. The 'even though' is the tell — she knows the study group was valid, but the guilt arrived anyway.

snippets.quote: I love that you're independent, it just means when you choose me it means more
snippets.reasoning: This is the load-bearing seedling. He praises her independence and immediately converts it into a resource for him — her choosing him becomes more valuable because she's independent. The structure installs a quiet obligation: to prove her independence is real, she must keep choosing him over it.

snippets.quote: I think that was a compliment?
snippets.reasoning: She cannot tell whether she was complimented or assigned a role. The question mark does the work — she absorbed the line, smiled probably, and is now privately unsure what happened. This is the rationalization beat that closes the load-bearing seedling without naming it.

snippets.quote: Is it weird that the part about the slow reply is still sitting with me a little?
snippets.reasoning: She asks about the smallest visible beat — the slow-reply episode — while the larger structural moves (the role assignment, the guilt installation, the premise-planting) sit unaddressed. 'Still sitting with me a little' is rationalization vocabulary minimizing what she actually registered.
